title: Backup completeness — unprotected workloads with regulated data
exclusions: Does NOT assess backup frequency; see UC-22.45.1 for RPO.
evidence: Daily 'backup_coverage_gap' writes unprotected-workload findings to compliance_summary.

compliance.assurance_rationale: CP-9 requires backup of system-level and user-level information — uncovered workloads fail the control.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIST 800-53 CP-9 (System backup) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.45.3: Backup completeness — unprotected workloads with regulated data.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.45.3' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.12 requires comprehensive backup; a gap is a direct finding.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that DORA Art.12 (Backup policies and recovery methods) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.45.3: Backup completeness — unprotected workloads with regulated data.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.45.3' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: ITGC backup and restore — coverage is the threshold question.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that SOX ITGC ITGC.Operations.Backup (Backup and restore) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.45.3: Backup completeness — unprotected workloads with regulated data.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.45.3' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: When a workload tagged regulated=true has no successful backup within the configured window (default 24h for tier-1), the UC fires naming the workload, tier, last_success.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Workloads with a successful backup within the window do NOT fire.

dataSources: See subcategory preamble. — supplemented by authentication logs, system audit trails, and configuration management database (CMDB) asset inventory exports
spl:
| rest /services/backup/inventory splunk_server=* | where regulated="true"
| stats max(last_success) as last_ok by workload tier
| lookup dr_tiers.csv tier OUTPUT expected_interval_hours
| eval hours_since=round((now()-last_ok)/3600,0)
| where hours_since>expected_interval_hours
| table tier workload last_ok hours_since expected_interval_hours
description: Cross-checks the backup inventory against the regulated-workload asset list. Any covered workload without a successful backup in its window becomes a finding.
value: Coverage gaps are the single most common backup finding at audit; continuous evidence eliminates surprise findings.
implementation: (1) Tag regulated workloads; (2) publish dr_tiers.csv; (3) run daily; (4) escalate via SOAR.
visualization: Heatmap of workload × day, bar chart of gaps by tier, single-value '% of regulated workloads protected'.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised security assessment activities** — Planned security control assessments, continuous monitoring scans, and Plan of Action and Milestones (POA&M) validation tests generate expected control deviation signals that are part of the Assessment & Authorisation process.

2. **System authorisation boundary changes** — During ATO re-authorisation, system boundary expansions, or inheritance model updates, temporary control gaps may appear in the monitoring baseline until the updated security plan is approved by the Authorising Official.

3. **Interconnection Security Agreement (ISA) activity** — Data exchanges with authorised external systems operating under signed ISAs produce cross-boundary traffic that triggers network monitoring alerts but is covered by the formal interconnection agreement.

4. **FIPS 199 impact-level re-categorisation** — When system categorisation changes (e.g., Moderate to High), the transition period produces control baseline mismatches until the enhanced controls are fully implemented per the approved implementation schedule.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `nist_800_53_exception_filter` KV Store lookup containing approved assessment schedule entries, POA&M IDs, ISA identifiers, and system re-categorisation project IDs. The correlation search filters against this lookup and suppresses matches with valid exception entries.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Lookups**: `dr_tiers.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('dr_tiers.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `CP-9`. The control objective is: "Evidence that NIST 800-53 CP-9 (System backup) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.45.3: Backup completeness — unprotected workloads with regulated data." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into the relevant indexes under the relevant sourcetypes, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Tag regulated workloads. CIM-compliance tagging must be validated end-to-end: a downstream datamodel that does not accelerate because of a missing tag is a silent failure.

• **Hint 2:** publish dr_tiers.csv. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** run daily. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** escalate via SOAR. Automated remediation must be gated behind a defined approval workflow; compliance controls that take destructive action without human review are themselves a governance risk.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "Backup completeness — unprotected workloads with regulated data," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the NIST 800-53 control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This search enriches raw events with context from the lookup `dr_tiers.csv` — the enrichment table that adds compliance-relevant business context to raw events. The raw event stream alone cannot answer the compliance question because it does not carry the authoritative business attributes (`expected_interval_hours`) — these live in a register maintained outside the monitored system. The `| lookup` joins at search time on `tier`, making the search results only as accurate as the lookup's freshness.

Lookup ownership and freshness: This is the single most common failure mode for lookup-based compliance searches. The `dr_tiers.csv` table must be owned by a named team with a documented refresh cadence and staleness alerting. A minimum operational SLA is: (1) lookup refreshed at a cadence matching the risk domain (daily for access control, weekly for inventory, quarterly for vendor registers), (2) automated alert when the lookup has not been rewritten in 2x the refresh interval, (3) a row-count sanity check (alert if row count changes by more than 20% between refreshes — catches truncation bugs in upstream exports).

Case sensitivity: Splunk's lookup matching is case-sensitive by default. If the raw event field and the lookup key differ in case (e.g. `Account_Name=JSMITH` vs lookup `samaccountname=jsmith`), the join silently returns null. Normalize both sides with `| eval tier = lower(tier)` before the lookup, or set `case_sensitive_match = false` in the lookup's `transforms.conf` stanza. Search across the full search range.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.45.3: Backup completeness — unprotected workloads with regulated d]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h@h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.45.3,reg=NIST_800-53
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `expected_interval_hours`, `hours_since`, `last_ok`, `tier`, `workload`. Run:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table expected_interval_hours, hours_since, last_ok, tier, workload
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `dr_tiers.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup dr_tiers.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.45.3: NIST 800-53 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Heatmap Of Workload × Day (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Bar Chart Of Gaps By Tier (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Single-Value '% Of Regulated Workloads Protected' (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** Check the lookup's case sensitivity (Splunk lookups are case-sensitive by default) and null handling — a null on the input side produces a silent miss, not an error.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NIST 800-53-specific failure modes:**

• Control baseline selection: NIST 800-53 has Low/Moderate/High/Privacy baselines via FIPS 199 categorisation. A detection written to a High baseline threshold produces false alarms against Low-categorised systems. Join with the system categorisation register `nist_800_53_system_categorization.csv` before thresholding.

• CA-7 continuous monitoring cadence: specifies 'ongoing' authorisation activities. Detections supporting CA-7 must produce metrics at the organisation's ConMon cadence (monthly is common for most agencies) — weekly fires are fine but not themselves evidence of monthly compliance.

• Control inheritance: in a cloud-hybrid deployment, some controls are inherited from the Cloud Service Provider. Detections must not flag 'missing' controls the CSP provides (documented in the SSP via Control Inheritance matrix); reference the SSP appendix and suppress matching findings.

• SP 800-53 Rev 5 restructure: controls were renumbered between Rev 4 and Rev 5. Detections that cite Rev 4 numbering (e.g. AC-2.12) may not match the SSP if the organisation has migrated. Store both Rev numbers in the UC metadata and reconcile.
grandmaExplanation: We help you show backups were tested and restorable when regulators or disaster recovery need proof.
